danger_signals.visual_form: Sprite representation of monsters on the overland map
danger_signals.meaning: Threats can be seen and temporarily avoided; danger is telegraphed

danger_signals.visual_form: Descending through 8-level dungeon floors with increasingly dense/detailed enemy sprites
danger_signals.meaning: Deeper levels = exponentially greater danger; visual complexity indicates threat escalation

danger_signals.visual_form: Guard sprites appear when player commits crimes (theft, murder)
danger_signals.meaning: Legal consequences; guards spawn in parties of 8 and are visually intimidating
Hidden danger; player must assess risk vs. reward in treasure acquisition

danger_signals.visual_form: Hostile ship sprites on water before player obtains a boat
danger_signals.meaning: Water-based threat; must be defeated to progress and gain naval access

safety_signals.visual_form: Clustered building sprites with NPC characters and Lord British
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zones for trading, leveling, and information gathering

safety_signals.visual_form: Central castle sprite with recognizable architectural design
safety_signals.meaning: Ultimate safe zone; provides leveling services and cannot be defeated in normal combat
Resource nodes for recovery; some heal, some cure status, indicating safe restorative points

safety_signals.visual_form: Torch sprites or illuminated areas in dark dungeon environments
safety_signals.meaning: Ability to see = reduced ambush danger; darkness indicates navigation hazard

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Party members represented as single icon during overworld travel; split into four distinct character sprites during tactical combat
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Party icon moves across overland map; appears in towns and dungeons

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Guards appear as uniformed sprites spawned when crimes committed; cannot be bribed once combat starts
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle location; guards stationed at posts; guard parties consist of 8 sprites

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Pirate ships patrol water regions; become player-controllable vessel once defeated
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Water-based encounters; naval presence indicates anarchic/hostile territory

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Final boss requiring all four marks and prayer cards to defeat; represents culmination of 'Age of Darkness' narrative
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Accessible only by boat; island of Ambrosia serves as gateway; requires complete ritual preparation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Overland monster encounters are avoidable; dungeon enemy density is light in upper levels; towns are primary interaction hubs
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Monsters on overworld can be outrun on foot; deeper dungeons are impassable without stronger equipment and levels

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Horse travel becomes available (faster sprite movement); more weapon/armor variety visible in NPC inventories; dungeon navigation becomes safer
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can engage in more criminal actions (bribery, stealing) as power increases; guards remain threats but battles are winnable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Boat becomes accessible; naval sprite appears; final dungeon/island becomes visually distinct; marks and prayer cards are acquired through visual dungeon rewards
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can engage Exodus; world-state progression becomes linear toward final confrontation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Guard sprites spawn in response to theft or violence; visual party composition shifts as player may be hunted
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: 8-guard parties pursue player; extended combat sequences; cannot rest safely in towns

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dense enemy sprites at depth; treasure chest visual indicators; trapped chest indistinguishability; fountain sprites signal restorative stations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Random enemy encounters; risk/reward assessment for chest opening; mandatory torch/light usage

environmental_storytelling.example: Eight-level dungeons with increasing visual enemy density and complexity communicate the escalating danger and importance of mark acquisition

environmental_storytelling.visual_technique: Mountain peaks, forests, and walls obscure hidden content; overland map design deliberately hides treasures, secret paths, and NPCs to create exploration incentive

environmental_storytelling.example: Bribe, steal, and fight commands on townspeople have immediate visual consequences (guard spawning, combat initiation) that communicate social order and legal framework
Character armor and weapon sprites change appearance based on inventory; visual upgrades communicate progression and class identity
Progression from foot → horse → boat as sprite-based travel speed increase; unlocking boat requires defeating pirate ships, visually marking naval dominance
Dungeon completion yields visible mark sprites; collection of all four marks visualized in inventory, building toward final confrontation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Visible on map; can be avoided with faster travel (horse); will pursue if engaged; behavior changes based on character level vs. monster power
Random encounter generation; density increases with depth; behavior determined by dungeon level rather than party level; serve as gate-keepers for marks
Stationed at posts; spawn in parties of 8 when crimes committed; always prioritize player party; cannot be bribed once combat initiated
Stationed in castle; provides leveling service; cannot be killed by conventional combat (can only be temporarily disabled by cannon from ship); restores upon location re-entry
Appear on water before player boat acquisition; must be defeated for naval travel; sprite behavior indicates territorial water control

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Unified during travel / Tactical during combat
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Single icon overworld; four separate sprites in battle mode; individual character turns in tactical sequence; behavioral split communicates mode transition

notes: Ultima: Exodus (NES) is a loose port/remake of Ultima III with completely redesigned graphics and music tailored to NES hardware. The game communicates world-state primarily through enemy visibility on overworld maps, dungeon depth progression, character progression visualization (equipment sprites), and availability of transportation methods. Faction identity is weak in NES port; primary antagonistic force is Exodus and chaos-aligned pirates. Safety is indicated by proximity to towns and Lord British. The visual design emphasizes exploration through deliberate map obscuration (mountains, forests, walls hiding content) and dungeon danger through sprite density escalation. The party mechanic (single icon → four sprites in combat) creates visual mode differentiation. Criminal consequences are immediately manifested through guard spawning, communicating a functional legal system. Trap chests and fountain effects communicate risk assessment mechanics through visual indistinguishability and beneficial sprite placement.
sources_used: tcrf - Ultima: Exodus (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor
